doc_metadata.document:
Kilo is a very small terminal text editor by Salvatore Sanfilippo, also known as antirez. Its purpose is to provide a usable editor in less than one thousand lines of C while remaining easy to study and adapt. The README describes it as alpha-stage software written in a few hours from ideas in the author's load81 and linenoise projects, and encourages people to use it as a starting point for more advanced terminal editors or command-line interfaces.

The program is intentionally self-contained. It does not use curses or another terminal UI library; instead, `kilo.c` talks directly to the terminal with fairly standard VT100-style escape sequences. The Makefile builds a single `kilo` executable from `kilo.c` with a C99 compiler and warning flags. The editor is started as `kilo <filename>`, and `main` exits with a usage message unless exactly one filename is provided.

At startup, `main` initializes the global editor state, selects syntax highlighting based on the filename, opens the requested file, enables raw terminal mode, sets a help message, and then loops forever refreshing the screen and processing one keypress at a time. Most editor state lives in the global `struct editorConfig E`, including cursor position, scroll offsets, terminal dimensions, file rows, dirty state, filename, status message, and selected syntax. Each file line is represented by an `erow`, which stores the original characters, a rendered version used for display, and syntax-highlight metadata.

Kilo's core user-facing features are basic file editing, saving, quitting, searching, scrolling, and C/C++ syntax highlighting. The README lists the main shortcuts: Ctrl-S saves, Ctrl-Q quits, and Ctrl-F searches. `editorProcessKeypress` is the central dispatcher for those commands. It inserts printable characters by default, handles Enter and deletion, moves the cursor with arrow and page keys, ignores Ctrl-C to avoid accidental data loss, and requires repeated Ctrl-Q presses before quitting when the file has unsaved changes.

File loading and saving are deliberately simple. `editorOpen` reads the target file line by line with `getline`, strips trailing newline or carriage-return characters, and inserts each line into the in-memory row array. If the file does not exist, opening is treated as a new empty file rather than an error. `editorSave` converts the row array back into one buffer, opens or creates the target file, truncates it to the new length, writes the buffer, clears the dirty flag on success, and reports status to the user.

The screen renderer builds a temporary append buffer of terminal escape sequences and visible text, then writes it to standard output in one call to reduce flicker. `editorRefreshScreen` hides the cursor, draws file rows or tildes for empty space, draws a two-line status area, positions the cursor according to scroll offsets and tab expansion, and shows the cursor again. Terminal raw mode is enabled with termios so keypresses can be read directly, and window size changes are handled by updating the stored screen dimensions and refreshing the display.

Search is interactive and lives in `editorFind`. As the user types a query, Kilo updates the status line, refreshes the screen, searches through rendered rows, scrolls to the current match, and temporarily marks the matched text with the `HL_MATCH` highlight type. Arrow keys move to the next or previous match, Enter accepts the current location, and Escape restores the cursor and scroll position from before search.

In short, Kilo is a compact single-file C editor meant to demonstrate the essential pieces of a terminal editor: raw-mode input, VT100 output, an editable in-memory row model, file persistence, status UI, search, and syntax highlighting. Its main value is educational and hackable rather than completeness.
